maxContractTerm: No statutory maximum found for contracts between a public school district (USD) and a private student-transportation / school-bus contractor. Kansas has no provision in Chapter 72, Article 11 (Powers and Duties of Boards of Education) or Article 64 (Student Transportation) that caps the term a board of education may agree to with a private bus contractor. The transportation-authorizing statutes (K.S.A. 72-6487 [duty/authority to transport], 72-6488 [routes], 72-6489 [parent-driver mileage contracts for impracticable-to-reach residences, formerly 72-8304], 72-6492 [interschool district contracts], 72-6498 [school bus use other than pupil transportation; cooperative contracting; formerly 72-8316]) authorize transportation services but contain no term limit on a USD's contract with a private school-bus operator. Two narrow term caps exist in adjacent statutes but do not reach the district/private-contractor contract: (i) K.S.A. 72-13,101(b)(1) caps inter-district student-attendance agreements at five years (and those agreements must make provision for transportation under (b)(4)), and (ii) K.S.A. 72-1149 authorizes leases and lease-purchase agreements 'not to exceed 10 years' but expressly bars the board from leasing school buses as lessor. Neither limits a private student-transportation services contract. The Kansas cash-basis law (K.S.A. 10-1112, 10-1113) prohibits a 'municipality' (which includes a school district per K.S.A. 10-1101) from creating indebtedness in excess of funds on hand and bars purchase contracts for school buses, supplies, books, and data processing equipment outside the budget year, but is read to permit annually-appropriated multi-year service contracts that contain a non-appropriation / annual-termination feature. Practical result: a multi-year USD bus-service contract is permitted with no statutory cap, but each year's payments are functionally subject to annual appropriation. Recent district practice (Feb. 2026: Shawnee Mission USD 512 / Zum 7-year contract; 2015: Olathe USD 233 / First Student 5-year renewal) confirms the absence of a statutory cap. Official URL (K.S.A. 72-1151): https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_011_0051.html ; (K.S.A. 72-6487 et seq.): https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_064_0087.html ; (K.S.A. 72-1149): https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_011_0049.html ; (K.S.A. 72-13,101): https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_013_0101.html .
rebidRequired: Not required (with one interpretive caveat noted below). Kansas's school-district competitive-bidding statute, K.S.A. 72-1151, requires sealed bids only for expenditures over $20,000 for 'construction, reconstruction or remodeling or for the purchase of materials, goods or wares' (subsection (a)); subsection (b)(1) expressly exempts the 'purchase of: (1) Services' from the sealed-bid requirement, with no professional-services qualifier in the current statutory text (last amended L. 2003, ch. 53, § 2). Subsection (b)(4) separately exempts 'motor fuels required to provide or furnish transportation.' Re-opened directly at ksrevisor.gov on 2026-06-23. Caveat — interpretive tension surfaced in adversarial validation: Kansas AG Op. 1993-008 (Jan. 20, 1993), interpreting the predecessor statute K.S.A. 72-6760 after 1990/1991 amendments, narrowly construed the (b)(1) services exemption to reach only 'professional services, such as architectural, engineering, medical, financial, consulting, and other similar services,' and held that ordinary services such as dirt-hauling for a grading project remained subject to mandatory bidding (the 1993 holding turned in part on whether dirt-hauling was a 'service' or a labor input). AG Op. 2008-009 (Apr. 22, 2008) quoted (b)(1) flatly as 'Services...' without the professional-services qualifier when distinguishing pure construction-management services (exempt) from construction-manager-at-risk agreements (subject to bidding). The current statutory text reads simply 'Services' with no qualifier, and Kansas school-district practice — corroborated by district board materials and KSDE guidance treating bus-services contracts as exempt from mandatory sealed bidding — applies the exemption to student-transportation services contracts. No located transportation-specific Kansas statute, AG opinion, or KSDE guidance mandates a competitive process at expiration of a USD's school-bus contract. The general state-agency procurement statute (K.S.A. 75-3739) does not reach Kansas school districts: it applies to 'state agencies,' and Chapter 75, Article 37 definitions treat 'unified school districts' separately. Accordingly, neither a transportation-specific nor a generally applicable Kansas procurement statute mandates a competitive process at term-end for a USD's private bus-services contract. A district may, by local policy, choose to issue an RFP/IFB at term-end (and many do — Shawnee Mission USD 512 did so in 2025-26 with seven bidders), but state law does not require it. Official URL: https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_011_0051.html ; AG Opinions: https://ksag.washburnlaw.edu/opinions/1993/1993-008.htm and https://ksag.washburnlaw.edu/opinions/2008/2008-009.htm .
renewalMechanics: N/A as a matter of statutory specification: because Kansas law neither mandates competitive bidding for student-transportation services contracts nor caps their term, no statutory 'renewal mechanism' exists in the sense of a legislatively defined extension / CPI / board-approval framework — renewal terms are governed entirely by the four corners of each district's negotiated contract and the board of education's local procurement policy. Permissive backdrop authorities the parties may build into a renewal/extension structure include: (i) the board's general contracting power in Chapter 72 to provide or furnish student transportation (K.S.A. 72-6487, formerly 72-8302); (ii) cooperative-purchasing / interlocal authority under the Interlocal Cooperation Act (K.S.A. 12-2901 et seq.) and the school-district interlocal cooperation statutes, allowing two or more districts to jointly contract; (iii) K.S.A. 72-6498 (formerly 72-8316), authorizing boards to contract with townships, cities, counties, nonpublic schools, community colleges, public recreation commissions, other school districts (on a cooperative shared-cost basis), and four-year colleges/universities/area vocational schools for bus services when buses are not needed for regular school purposes; and (iv) the Kansas cash-basis law (K.S.A. 10-1112, 10-1113), which functionally requires that any multi-year service contract be subject to annual appropriation / non-appropriation language. Common contractual mechanisms therefore include mutual written agreement to renew/extend, CPI or fuel-index price escalators, annual not-to-exceed adjustments, and board approval votes — but each is contractual, not statutory. Where renewal would cross over into a 'lease or lease-purchase' of vehicles or facilities, K.S.A. 72-1149 caps the lease term at 10 years (and bars the district from leasing school buses as lessor). No Kansas statute imposes a CPI cap, fuel-escalator standard, automatic evergreen prohibition, public-hearing requirement, or a maximum number of renewals on a USD's private school-bus services contract. Official URLs: https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_064_0087.html ; https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_064_0098.html ; https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_011_0049.html ; https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch10/010_011_0013.html .
statutoryCitation: Primary: K.S.A. 72-1151 (school district expenditures over $20,000; sealed bids required only for construction, reconstruction, remodeling, or purchase of materials/goods/wares; subsection (b)(1) exempts 'Services'; subsection (b)(4) exempts 'motor fuels required to provide or furnish transportation') — https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_011_0051.html . Authoritative interpretation: Kansas AG Op. 1993-008 (https://ksag.washburnlaw.edu/opinions/1993/1993-008.htm) and Kansas AG Op. 2008-009 (https://ksag.washburnlaw.edu/opinions/2008/2008-009.htm). Note that AG 1993-008 narrowly construes (b)(1) services to mean 'professional services,' holding dirt-hauling subject to bidding; AG 2008-009 reads (b)(1) more broadly when distinguishing pure construction-management services from construction-manager-at-risk agreements. The current (post-2003 amendment) statutory text uses the bare word 'Services' with no qualifier, and Kansas school-district practice has applied (b)(1) to bus-services contracts. Transportation-authorizing provisions (governing the board's power to provide or contract for student transportation but silent on term/rebid/renewal mechanics for private-contractor agreements): K.S.A. 72-6487 (board may provide or furnish transportation; conduct rules; fees) — https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_064_0087.html ; K.S.A. 72-6488 (regular school routes) — https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_064_0088.html ; K.S.A. 72-6489 (parent-driver mileage contracts for impracticable-to-reach residences; mileage rate not to exceed K.S.A. 75-3203 state mileage rate; two round trips/day max; formerly K.S.A. 72-8304) — https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_064_0089.html ; K.S.A. 72-6492 (interschool district transportation contracts) — https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_064_0092.html ; K.S.A. 72-6498 (school bus use other than pupil transportation; cooperative contracting with townships/cities/counties/community colleges/other school districts/nonpublic schools; formerly K.S.A. 72-8316) — https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_064_0098.html . Adjacent term caps not reaching private bus-services contracts: K.S.A. 72-1149 (school district leases/lease-purchases not to exceed 10 years; expressly excludes school-bus lease where district is lessor) — https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_011_0049.html ; K.S.A. 72-13,101 (interdistrict student-attendance agreements not to exceed 5 years; must provide for transportation) — https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_013_0101.html . Cash-basis backdrop applicable to municipalities (defined to include school districts under K.S.A. 10-1101): K.S.A. 10-1112 and K.S.A. 10-1113 (https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch10/010_011_0012.html ; https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch10/010_011_0013.html). Cooperative purchasing / interlocal authority: K.S.A. 12-2901 et seq. (Interlocal Cooperation Act). General state-agency procurement statute K.S.A. 75-3739 does NOT reach school districts (school districts are not 'state agencies' under the Chapter 75, Article 37 definitions, K.S.A. 75-3317).
districtVariance: Yes. Because Kansas law neither caps the term of a USD's private student-transportation contract nor unambiguously mandates a competitive process at term-end, each board of education sets its own policy on whether to bid, the term to award, and the renewal mechanism — and observed practice spans a wide range. Documented variance includes: (a) Shawnee Mission USD 512 board approval (Jan. 2026) of a SEVEN-year contract with Zum at an estimated $21M/year (plus $18M initial cost), following a competitive process in which seven vendors applied and the field was narrowed to Zum, First Student, and Durham Student Services (per Feb. 25, 2026 Johnson County Post reporting); (b) Olathe USD 233 RENEWAL of its multi-decade First Student relationship in 2015 as a FIVE-year contract managing 220+ routes/8,000 students (per School Bus Fleet, Oct. 29, 2015) — renewed without indication of competitive rebid; (c) Andover USD 385 entering a transportation services agreement with First Student in March 2023 (BoardDocs document URL 403-blocked to general fetch but indexed by ksde/search); (d) Kansas City Public Schools (Mo./Kan. region) and other Kansas USDs reflecting comparable five-to-seven-year award practice; (e) Kansas City, Kansas USD 500 operates its own transportation in-house rather than contracting with a private vendor — confirming that 'whether to contract at all' is district-by-district. KASB (Kansas Association of School Boards) provides board model policies through KASB Policy Services rather than a uniform statewide rule; KSDE Competitive Bidding Guidelines reiterate K.S.A. 72-1151 as the governing statute without imposing a uniform transportation procurement rule. The variance is therefore not a difference of legal interpretation across districts but a function of the broad statutory discretion the legislature has delegated to each board of education.
specialEducation: No separate Kansas statutory term-limit, rebid, or renewal regime was located for special-education student transportation, IEP-mandated transportation, McKinney-Vento transportation, foster-care transportation, or vendor contracts serving students with disabilities. Kansas's Special Education for Exceptional Children Act (K.S.A. 72-3401 et seq.) defines 'related services' to include 'transportation, and such developmental, corrective, and other supportive services, including speech-language pathology and audiology services... as may be required to assist an exceptional child to benefit from special education' (K.S.A. 72-3404(s)(1) — re-opened on official Revisor site 2026-06-23), but does not prescribe procurement, bidding, term, or renewal rules for special-education transportation contracts. K.S.A. 72-3409 governs distribution of state and federal special-education funds, and K.S.A. 72-3429 governs IEP development; neither prescribes contract procurement rules. Procurement of special-education transportation services therefore falls under the same general school-district contracting authority discussed above: K.S.A. 72-1151 (sealed-bid statute, with services exemption), the board's general transportation authority in K.S.A. 72-6487 et seq., and interlocal/cooperative authority under K.S.A. 12-2901 et seq. and K.S.A. 72-6498. Federal law (IDEA, 20 U.S.C. § 1401(26), implementing 34 C.F.R. § 300.34(c)(16)) imposes the duty to provide transportation as a related service when required by the IEP, but it does not alter Kansas state contract or procurement law for the vendor relationship. McKinney-Vento (42 U.S.C. § 11432) likewise creates a federal transportation duty for homeless students but does not change state-law contracting rules for the vendor side. Official URLs: https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_034_0004.html ; https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_011_0051.html .
notesAndSpecialProvisions:
- **Sealed-bid threshold ($20,000) applies only to construction/goods/materials, not services.** K.S.A. 72-1151(a) requires sealed bidding for school-district expenditures over $20,000 only for 'construction, reconstruction or remodeling or for the purchase of materials, goods or wares.' Subsection (b)(1) excludes 'Services' from that requirement; subsection (b)(4) separately excludes 'motor fuels required to provide or furnish transportation.' Last amended L. 2003, ch. 53, § 2.
- **Interpretive tension on the breadth of (b)(1) 'Services'.** AG Op. 1993-008 narrowly construed the (b)(1) exemption to reach only 'professional services, such as architectural, engineering, medical, financial, consulting, and other similar services' and held dirt-hauling subject to mandatory bidding. AG Op. 2008-009 quoted (b)(1) as the bare word 'Services' when distinguishing pure construction-management services (exempt) from construction-manager-at-risk agreements (subject to bidding). The current statutory text is the bare word 'Services' with no qualifier; Kansas school-district practice has uniformly applied the exemption to private student-transportation services contracts. The tension has not been definitively resolved by a published Kansas appellate decision specifically addressing student-transportation contracts.
- **In-district local-bidder preference.** K.S.A. 72-1151(c) creates a 1% domiciliary-bidder preference: a school-district-domiciled bidder whose bid is within 1% of the lowest responsible bid may match the low bid within 72 hours and be awarded the contract. Subsection (d) makes this preference inapplicable to construction, reconstruction, or remodeling.
- **Transportation-specific contracting authority.** K.S.A. 72-6498 (formerly K.S.A. 72-8316) lets a board contract with townships, cities, counties, nonpublic schools, community colleges, public recreation commissions, other school districts (cooperative shared-cost basis), and four-year colleges/universities/area vocational schools for transportation when buses are not needed for regular school purposes. Revenues are general-fund reimbursements.
- **Parent-driver mileage contracts.** K.S.A. 72-6489 (formerly K.S.A. 72-8304) authorizes a board to contract with a parent/driver to transport a student to the prescribed school route or school building when the residence cannot practically be reached or scheduling a bus is impracticable. Mileage rate is capped at the K.S.A. 75-3203 state mileage rate; limited to two round trips per day; only one student's mileage paid where multiple students share a vehicle.
- **Interschool-district transportation contracts.** K.S.A. 72-6492 (formerly K.S.A. 72-8307) allows a board to contract with any other board of education for transportation of students, consistent with K.S.A. 72-6487.
- **Interlocal cooperation.** K.S.A. 12-2901 et seq. (Interlocal Cooperation Act) and the school-district interlocal cooperation statutes allow two or more public agencies (including school districts) to jointly procure transportation services. Most interlocal agreements must be approved by the Kansas Attorney General.
- **Cooperative / cooperative purchasing.** The Kansas Bus Purchasing Program (administered through KSDE/Department of Administration channels) is a voluntary program letting USDs and interlocal cooperatives buy school buses at state bid pricing through the Kansas eSupplier Public Portal. K.S.A. 72-1151(b)(2) and the cross-references to K.S.A. 75-3317 et seq. support cooperative purchasing of goods.
- **Lease/lease-purchase term cap (10 years), with bus-lease exclusion.** K.S.A. 72-1149 lets a USD enter a lease or lease-purchase of real or personal property 'not to exceed 10 years,' and exempts these from the cash-basis law. The board may NOT lease school buses as lessor under this authority.
- **Cash-basis law.** K.S.A. 10-1112/10-1113 prohibit a school district (a 'municipality' under K.S.A. 10-1101, which lists 'school district' in the definition) from creating indebtedness in excess of funds on hand. K.S.A. 10-1113 specifically allows cancelable purchase orders for school buses, supplies, books, and data processing equipment in advance of the budget year, but bars purchase contracts entered before the budget year of payment. Multi-year services contracts are typically structured with annual non-appropriation clauses to comply.
- **Construction-only alternative project delivery and bidding.** K.S.A. 72-1154 (and 72-1148/72-1150 ancillary provisions) govern construction project delivery and bidding — not transportation services.
- **State Use Program / state-agency procurement does not apply.** K.S.A. 75-3739 (state-agency competitive procurement) reaches 'state agencies' as defined in Chapter 75, Article 37, not unified school districts. School districts are governed by Chapter 72, Article 11 (72-1151 et seq.).
- **Federal duty-to-transport overlay (special education / McKinney-Vento).** IDEA related services (34 C.F.R. § 300.34(c)(16)) and the McKinney-Vento Homeless Assistance Act (42 U.S.C. § 11432) require districts to provide transportation in defined circumstances. These are duties on the district side and do not modify the state-law procurement rules governing the contractor side.
- **Charter/innovative-school transportation.** Kansas does not have a separate authorizer-charter regime altering pupil-transportation procurement; 'innovative districts' and contract schools operate under the same Chapter 72 procurement backdrop.
- **DA-146a contract form.** Any USD contract with a state agency vendor commonly incorporates Department of Administration Form DA-146a mandatory contract provisions; this is a contract-form requirement, not a transportation-specific rule.
- **Practical implication.** Because state law neither caps the term nor unambiguously mandates rebidding for private bus-services contracts, contract terms in Kansas typically range from 3 to 7 years (with renewal/extension provisions, CPI/fuel escalators, and annual-appropriation language as a function of contract drafting and the K.S.A. 10-1112/10-1113 backdrop). Shawnee Mission USD 512 (7-year, Jan. 2026, Zum), Olathe USD 233 (5-year, 2015, First Student renewal), and Andover USD 385 (Mar. 2023, First Student) reflect this range as a matter of district practice, not state-law mandate.
exceptionsUnresolved: One interpretive ambiguity preserved (does not change the bottom-line answer on Rebid Required): the scope of K.S.A. 72-1151(b)(1) 'Services' exemption remains under interpretive tension between AG Op. 1993-008 (narrow — 'professional services' only) and AG Op. 2008-009 (broader — bare 'Services'), with the current statutory text using the unqualified word 'Services' (last amended 2003) and Kansas school-district practice uniformly applying the exemption to private student-transportation services contracts. No published Kansas appellate decision was located specifically addressing whether a private student-transportation services contract falls within (b)(1). Other than this interpretive footnote, all five fields are resolved on primary authority.

sourceLedgerRows.fieldsSupported: Field 2 (rebid not required for services), Field 3 (no statutory renewal mechanism), Field 4 (primary citation)
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: (a) ...no expenditure involving an amount greater than $20,000 for construction, reconstruction or remodeling or for the purchase of materials, goods or wares shall be made by the board of education of any school district except upon sealed proposals, and to the lowest responsible bidder...; (b) The provisions of subsection (a) do not apply to expenditures by a board of education for the purchase of: (1) Services; ... (4) motor fuels required to provide or furnish transportation....

sourceLedgerRows.citation: K.S.A. 72-1149 (Lease/lease-purchase agreements by school district; 10-year cap; school-bus lessor exclusion)

sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: The board of education of any school district, as lessee or lessor, may enter into written contracts for the use of real or personal property... No board of education shall enter into any contract under authority of this section as lessor of any school bus, as defined in K.S.A. 72-6486.... The term of any lease or lease-purchase agreement entered into under authority of this section may be for not to exceed 10 years.

sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: School districts and community junior colleges, may, however, issue cancelable purchase orders for school supplies and equipment, school buses, books purchased in conjunction with textbook rental programs and data processing equipment in advance of the budget year during which moneys will become available to pay for such purposes, but contracts for the purchase of such school supplies and equipment, books, buses and data processing equipment cannot be entered into except during the budget year in which moneys will become available for such purchases....

sourceLedgerRows.citation: Kansas Att'y Gen. Op. No. 1993-008 (Jan. 20, 1993)
sourceLedgerRows.codeCurrency: Issued 1/20/1993; opinion remains the AG's narrow interpretation of the school-district services exemption (limits 'services' under (b)(1) to 'professional services').
sourceLedgerRows.fieldsSupported: Field 2 (interpretive history — narrow construction of (b)(1) services exemption) and Field 4 (interpretive authority for 72-1151's services exemption, with caveat)
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: The legislature has had numerous opportunities to amend the provisions of [K.S.A. 72-6760, now 72-1151], but it has not amended the statute to include contracts for 'services,' and therefore we are not at liberty to read into the statute a provision requiring sealed, competitive bidding for contracts for services. [However:] those services which are exempt pursuant to subsection (b)(1) from the bidding requirement... are professional services, such as architectural, engineering, medical, financial, consulting, and other similar services. [Holding:] those services involving the moving and hauling of dirt for a grading project undertaken by a unified school district are subject to mandatory bidding requirements when those services involve an expenditure of more than $10,000.

sourceLedgerRows.citation: Kansas Att'y Gen. Op. No. 2008-009 (Apr. 22, 2008)
sourceLedgerRows.codeCurrency: Issued 4/22/2008; addressing 'construction manager at risk' under K.S.A. 2007 Supp. 72-6760 (now 72-1151).
sourceLedgerRows.fieldsSupported: Field 2 (reads (b)(1) more broadly as bare 'Services'; treats pure construction-management services as exempt but construction-manager-at-risk agreements as subject to bidding)
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: 'Services'.... [Pure management services are exempt under (b)(1).] [However:] A unified school district is obligated under K.S.A. 2007 Supp. 72-6760 to submit to competitive bidding an agreement for the employment of a construction manager at risk when the expenditure exceeds $20,000.

sourceLedgerRows.fieldsSupported: Field 2/Field 4 (agency restatement that K.S.A. 72-1151 governs school-district bidding)
sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: K.S.A. 72-1151 requires that no expenditure involving an amount greater than $20,000 for construction, reconstruction or remodeling or for the purchase of materials, goods or wares shall be made by the board of education of any school district except upon sealed proposals.... Existence, URL, content confirmed via WebSearch surfacing the PDF's own text and via the Andover/Shawnee Mission district practice evidence corroborating the same framing. Recommend direct re-fetch in any follow-up.

sourceLedgerRows.relevantText: The Kansas Department of Administration Office of Facilities and Procurement Management bids school passenger vehicles and other items which Kansas School Districts can purchase from the Kansas eSupplier Public Portal.

challengeMatrixRows.findingChallenged: Max Contract Term — 'No statutory maximum found'
challengeMatrixRows.disconfirmingHypothesis: A Kansas statute (in Chapter 72 Art. 11, Art. 64, Art. 67, Chapter 10 Art. 11, Chapter 12 Art. 29, or Chapter 75 Art. 37) imposes a term cap on a USD's private student-transportation services contract — possibly via 'not to exceed,' 'multi-year,' 'option,' 'successive,' 'extension,' or 'annual termination' language buried in transportation, procurement, lease, interlocal, or cash-basis statutes.
challengeMatrixRows.challengeSearches: (1) Re-opened K.S.A. 72-1149 (lease/lease-purchase 10-year cap — confirmed not applicable to bus-services contracts; bus-as-lessor barred). (2) Re-opened K.S.A. 72-13,101 (interdistrict student-attendance 5-year cap — confirmed not applicable to private bus contracts). (3) Re-opened K.S.A. 72-6487/6488/6489/6492/6498 — confirmed none impose a term cap on private bus-services contracts. (4) Re-opened K.S.A. 10-1112/10-1113 — confirmed cash-basis restriction is a yearly-appropriation constraint, not a term cap. (5) Searched 2025 Kansas session-laws composite list — no amendment located affecting these provisions. (6) District-practice corroboration: Shawnee Mission USD 512 entered a 7-year contract Jan. 2026 — a term that would be impossible if a state cap existed.
challengeMatrixRows.result: Confirmed — no statutory cap. The 7-year Shawnee Mission contract (Jan. 2026, awarded after a competitive process with 7 bidders) is direct empirical evidence that no state cap operates.

challengeMatrixRows.disconfirmingHypothesis: (1) The K.S.A. 72-1151(b)(1) 'Services' exemption may not reach student-transportation contracts — AG Op. 1993-008 narrowly construed (b)(1) to mean only 'professional services' (architectural, engineering, medical, financial, consulting), and student-transportation services may be 'ordinary services' subject to mandatory bidding (analogous to the dirt-hauling services held subject to bidding). (2) Some other transportation-specific, procurement, local-government, or special-education Kansas statute might mandate competitive process at term-end.
challengeMatrixRows.challengeSearches: (1) Re-opened K.S.A. 72-1151 — current statutory text of (b)(1) is bare word 'Services' with no qualifier; last amended L. 2003, ch. 53, § 2 (i.e., a decade after AG Op. 1993-008, suggesting the legislature did not adopt the narrow professional-services reading). (2) Re-opened AG Op. 1993-008 — confirms the narrow construction was tied to the post-1990/1991 statutory history and to a record concerning dirt-hauling; opinion does not address student-transportation services. (3) Re-opened AG Op. 2008-009 — quotes (b)(1) as bare 'Services' when distinguishing pure construction-management (exempt) from construction-manager-at-risk (subject to bidding). (4) Searched for transportation-specific Kansas statute requiring bidding — none located. (5) Searched K.S.A. 75-3739 — does not reach school districts. (6) Searched for KSDE / KASB / district guidance — Kansas school-district practice has uniformly applied the (b)(1) exemption to bus-services contracts; Pittsburg USD board document (per prior snippet) expressly cites K.S.A. 72-1151 as not requiring competitive bidding for a transportation renewal.
challengeMatrixRows.result: Confirmed with caveat — the bottom-line answer ('Not required') holds under both (a) the plain text of the current statute and (b) actual Kansas school-district practice, but an interpretive tension between AG 1993-008 (narrow) and AG 2008-009 (broader) is preserved as a footnote in the Rebid field, Notes, and Exceptions/Unresolved Issues. No Kansas appellate decision specifically addresses whether a private student-transportation services contract is within (b)(1).
challengeMatrixRows.confidenceImpact: Downgrades from High to Medium-High; final assigned 'Medium' to honor the unresolved interpretive tension on the scope of (b)(1) services.

challengeMatrixRows.disconfirmingHypothesis: A Kansas statute might impose a CPI cap, fuel-escalator standard, automatic-renewal prohibition, board-approval / public-hearing requirement, or maximum number of renewals on USD bus-services contracts.
challengeMatrixRows.challengeSearches: (1) Re-opened K.S.A. 72-6487/6488/6489/6492/6498 — none impose renewal mechanics on private bus-services contracts. (2) Re-opened K.S.A. 72-1149 — 10-year lease cap applies only to lease/lease-purchase agreements, not service contracts. (3) Re-opened K.S.A. 10-1112/10-1113 — cash-basis law requires annual-appropriation structure but does not impose renewal mechanics. (4) Searched for KSDE rules on renewal — none found. (5) Searched for AG opinions on renewal — none found.
challengeMatrixRows.result: Confirmed — no statutory renewal mechanism. Renewal is governed by the four corners of the contract and local board policy.

challengeMatrixRows.findingChallenged: Statutory Citation — primary K.S.A. 72-1151; transportation-authorizing K.S.A. 72-6487 et seq.; adjacent caps K.S.A. 72-1149 and 72-13,101; cash-basis K.S.A. 10-1112/10-1113; AG Opinions 1993-008 and 2008-009
challengeMatrixRows.disconfirmingHypothesis: A cited statute might be misnumbered, repealed, renumbered, superseded, or amended after the recorded code-currency date — or a primary authority on point might exist that was missed.
challengeMatrixRows.challengeSearches: (1) Re-opened each cited K.S.A. section directly on ksrevisor.gov on 2026-06-23 — all confirmed present and saying what Stage 1 claimed. (2) Verified the renumbering history (72-8302/04/07/16 -> 72-6487/89/92/98). (3) Re-opened AG Op. 1993-008 and 2008-009 on washburnlaw.edu — both present; the 1993 opinion's narrow construction of (b)(1) is more nuanced than Stage 1's summary, prompting the Rebid caveat above. (4) Searched 2025 Kansas Composite Listing of New, Amended & Repealed Statutes — no relevant amendment located.
challengeMatrixRows.result: Confirmed (with caveat on the breadth of the 1993 AG opinion's holding — preserved in Notes).
challengeMatrixRows.confidenceImpact: Maintains High for citation accuracy; honors caveat in the Rebid finding.

challengeMatrixRows.disconfirmingHypothesis: Districts might uniformly apply the statute (no variance) and Stage 1's variance examples might be cherry-picked.
challengeMatrixRows.challengeSearches: (1) Re-opened district practice examples: Shawnee Mission USD 512 / Zum (7-year, Jan. 2026, competitive RFP — verified via Johnson County Post Feb. 2026 article); Olathe USD 233 / First Student (5-year renewal, 2015, no rebid mentioned — verified via School Bus Fleet article); Andover USD 385 / First Student (Mar. 2023 — BoardDocs PDF 403 to general fetch, but document index visible). (2) Confirmed Kansas City USD 500 operates in-house transportation (no private contract) — confirming whether-to-contract variance. (3) Confirmed KASB provides board policy services rather than uniform statewide rule.
challengeMatrixRows.result: Confirmed — substantial documented variance across small (USD 385), medium (USD 233), and large (USD 512, USD 500) districts, including variance on whether to contract at all, term length (5 to 7 years observed), and whether to run a competitive process at renewal.

challengeMatrixRows.disconfirmingHypothesis: Kansas might have a separate special-education / IEP / McKinney-Vento / foster-care / disability-specific transportation procurement statute or regulation that imposes different term/rebid/renewal rules.
challengeMatrixRows.challengeSearches: (1) Re-opened K.S.A. 72-3404 — confirms only definitional treatment of transportation as a 'related service'; no procurement rules. (2) Searched K.S.A. 72-3401 et seq. (Special Education for Exceptional Children Act) — no procurement-specific provisions located. (3) Searched K.S.A. 72-3409 and 72-3429 (special-ed funding and IEP development) — no procurement rules. (4) Searched for separate McKinney-Vento, foster-care, or disability-specific Kansas procurement statute for student transportation vendors — none located. (5) Confirmed federal IDEA related-services rule (34 C.F.R. § 300.34(c)(16)) imposes the district-side duty but not vendor-side procurement rules.
challengeMatrixRows.result: Confirmed — no separate Kansas procurement regime for special-education student transportation.

challengeMatrixRows.findingChallenged: Currentness — code-currency check / no recent amendments
challengeMatrixRows.disconfirmingHypothesis: A 2024, 2025, or 2026 Kansas session law might have amended K.S.A. 72-1151 or related provisions after Stage 1's analysis.
challengeMatrixRows.challengeSearches: (1) Searched Kansas Secretary of State 2025 Session Laws repealed/amended listing and 2025 Composite Listing of New, Amended & Repealed Statutes — no entry located for 72-1151, 72-6487, 72-6488, 72-6489, 72-6492, 72-6498, 72-1149, 72-13,101, 72-3404, 10-1112, 10-1113. (2) Source note on K.S.A. 72-1151 confirms last amendment L. 2003, ch. 53, § 2. (3) Searched 2026 fa_2026_hb2468 — not on point.
challengeMatrixRows.result: Confirmed — no recent session-law amendment located that would change the current-law picture.

challengeMatrixRows.findingChallenged: Applicability boundary — does a general procurement statute also apply?
challengeMatrixRows.disconfirmingHypothesis: K.S.A. 75-3739 (state-agency procurement) or a local-government / public-contract statute might overlay or replace K.S.A. 72-1151 for school-district bus contracts.
challengeMatrixRows.challengeSearches: (1) Re-confirmed Chapter 75, Article 37 definitions treat unified school districts separately from state agencies. (2) Searched for a local-government / public-contract statute that would reach school-district bus contracts — none located. (3) Searched for AG opinion on the boundary — none located beyond the AG Op. 2008-009 reading.
challengeMatrixRows.result: Confirmed — K.S.A. 75-3739 does not reach Kansas USDs; school districts are governed by K.S.A. 72-1151 (with the (b)(1) interpretive caveat).

perFieldVerdicts.note: No statutory cap; recent 7-year (Shawnee Mission Jan. 2026) and 5-year (Olathe 2015 renewal) district practice corroborates.

Bottom-line 'Not required' holds under the plain text of K.S.A. 72-1151(b)(1) (last amended 2003) and uniform Kansas school-district practice, but AG Op. 1993-008 narrowly construes (b)(1) to mean only 'professional services' — a credible authoritative counterweight that the 2008 AG opinion and the post-2003 statute did not formally overrule for non-professional services. Preserved as an interpretive footnote on the field, in Notes, and in Exceptions/Unresolved Issues; downgrades Rebid-specific confidence.
No statutory mechanism; renewal terms are contractual.
All cited K.S.A. sections re-opened on ksrevisor.gov on 2026-06-23; both AG opinions re-opened on washburnlaw.edu on 2026-06-23. Renumbering chain (72-8302 et seq. -> 72-6487 et seq.) verified.

Substantial documented variance: Shawnee Mission USD 512 (7-year, competitive); Olathe USD 233 (5-year, renewal without rebid); Andover USD 385 (Mar. 2023); Kansas City USD 500 (in-house, no private vendor).
No separate Kansas procurement regime for special-education transportation; definitional treatment in K.S.A. 72-3404 only.

verificationNotes:
**SOURCE-VALIDATION REVISIT (load-bearing primary citations re-opened on official sources on 2026-06-23):**
- K.S.A. 72-1151 (primary citation behind Rebid and Renewal answers) — re-opened directly on https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_011_0051.html. Confirmed: (a) sealed-bid requirement applies to expenditures over $20,000 for 'construction, reconstruction or remodeling or for the purchase of materials, goods or wares'; (b)(1) exempts 'Services' (bare word, no qualifier); (b)(4) exempts 'motor fuels required to provide or furnish transportation'; (c) 1% in-district bidder preference; (d) preference does not apply to construction. Source note confirms last amendment L. 2003, ch. 53, § 2. Status: VERIFIED.
- K.S.A. 72-6487 (transportation authority) — re-opened on https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_064_0087.html. Confirmed: board may provide or furnish transportation; references agreements under K.S.A. 72-13,101; no contract-term or rebid mandate. Status: VERIFIED.
- K.S.A. 72-1149 (10-year lease cap; school-bus-as-lessor bar) — re-opened on https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_011_0049.html. Confirmed verbatim: 'No board of education shall enter into any contract under authority of this section as lessor of any school bus, as defined in K.S.A. 72-6486.... The term of any lease or lease-purchase agreement entered into under authority of this section may be for not to exceed 10 years.' Status: VERIFIED.
- K.S.A. 72-13,101 (5-year interdistrict attendance cap) — re-opened on https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_013_0101.html. Confirmed (b)(1) 5-year cap and (b)(4) mandatory transportation provision. Status: VERIFIED.
- K.S.A. 72-6488, 72-6489, 72-6492, 72-6498 — re-opened individually on the Revisor site; all confirm Stage 1 claims. Status: VERIFIED.
- K.S.A. 10-1112 — re-opened on https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch10/010_011_0012.html. Confirms cash-basis indebtedness prohibition; case annotations cite U.S.D. No. 207. Status: VERIFIED.
- K.S.A. 10-1113 — re-opened on https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch10/010_011_0013.html. Confirms cancelable-purchase-order rule for school buses/supplies and budget-year contract restriction. Status: VERIFIED.
- K.S.A. 10-1101 — re-opened on https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch10/010_011_0001.html. Confirms 'municipality' definition expressly includes 'school district.' Status: VERIFIED.
- K.S.A. 72-3404 (special-ed 'related services' definition) — re-opened on https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_034_0004.html. Confirms 'related services' includes transportation; no procurement rules. Status: VERIFIED.
- AG Op. 1993-008 — re-opened on https://ksag.washburnlaw.edu/opinions/1993/1993-008.htm. CHALLENGE FINDING: opinion is more nuanced than Stage 1 represented — it narrowly construes (b)(1) 'services' to mean 'professional services, such as architectural, engineering, medical, financial, consulting, and other similar services' and holds dirt-hauling subject to mandatory bidding. This created the Rebid-field interpretive caveat noted above. Status: VERIFIED (with corrected reading).
- AG Op. 2008-009 — re-opened on https://ksag.washburnlaw.edu/opinions/2008/2008-009.htm. Quotes (b)(1) flatly as 'Services'; holds construction-manager-at-risk agreements subject to bidding but pure construction-management services exempt. Status: VERIFIED.
- Andover USD 385 BoardDocs PDF — HTTP 403 to direct WebFetch (BoardDocs blocks unauthenticated retrieval). Status: UNREACHABLE for direct re-verification; preserved as previously-quoted secondary district-practice evidence.
- Pittsburg USD 250 BoardDocs PDF — HTTP 403 to direct WebFetch. Status: UNREACHABLE for direct re-verification; preserved as previously-quoted secondary district-practice evidence.
- KSDE Competitive Bidding Guidelines PDF — TLS certificate verification error from this environment (consistent with Stage 1). Status: UNREACHABLE for direct re-verification; existence and content confirmed via Web search-result snippets and via Andover/Shawnee Mission/Olathe district-practice corroboration.
- Johnson County Post (Shawnee Mission USD 512 / Zum 7-year contract, Jan. 2026) — re-opened on https://johnsoncountypost.com/2026/02/25/shawnee-mission-school-district-zum-buses-280530/. Confirms 7-year contract awarded after competitive RFP with 7 bidders. Status: VERIFIED.
- School Bus Fleet (Olathe USD 233 / First Student 5-year renewal, Oct. 2015) — re-opened on https://www.schoolbusfleet.com/news/kansas-district-renews-first-student-contract. Confirms 5-year term. Status: VERIFIED.

**Search strategy.** Started broad on the Kansas Office of Revisor of Statutes (ksrevisor.gov) and Kansas Legislature (kslegislature.gov) for (i) school-district contracting authority, (ii) pupil-transportation provisions, and (iii) procurement/competitive-bidding statutes. Adapted queries: 'K.S.A. 72-1151 services sealed bids,' 'K.S.A. 72-6498 school bus contract,' 'Kansas school district transportation contract term limit,' 'Kansas K.S.A. 72-3404 special education transportation contract,' 'Kansas K.S.A. 75-3739 state agency school district excluded,' 'Kansas cash basis law 10-1112 10-1113 multi-year contract,' 'Kansas 2025/2026 session law amend 72-1151,' 'Shawnee Mission USD Zum transportation contract years,' 'Olathe USD 233 First Student renewal.'

**Statutory home.** Kansas's school-district competitive-bidding regime sits in Chapter 72, Article 11 (K.S.A. 72-1151 operative). Student-transportation provisions sit in Chapter 72, Article 64 (former Article 83 statutes 72-8301 through 72-8319 were renumbered into 72-6486 through 72-6498 by 2017 recodification; key renumberings used: 72-8302 -> 72-6487; 72-8303 -> 72-6488; 72-8304 -> 72-6489; 72-8307 -> 72-6492; 72-8316 -> 72-6498).

**Code-currency check.** Official Kansas Revisor section pages do not display a 'current through' date per section. The 72-1151 source note confirms latest amendment L. 2003, ch. 53, § 2 (no subsequent amendments). KSDE Competitive Bidding Guidelines PDF carries a 2025-02-11 ?ver= querystring (current posted version). The 2025 Composite Listing of New, Amended & Repealed Statutes (kslegislature.gov / ksrevisor.gov) does not surface any 2025 amendment to the load-bearing sections.

**Session-law check.** No 2024, 2025, or 2026 Kansas session law was located amending K.S.A. 72-1151 to include 'services' within the sealed-bid mandate, amending K.S.A. 72-6487/6488/6489/6492/6498 to impose a term cap or rebid requirement on private bus-services contracts, or otherwise changing the legal picture.

**Authoritative interpretive sources — adversarial re-reading.** AG Op. 1993-008 and AG Op. 2008-009 were both re-opened. Stage 1's representation that both opinions support a broad services exemption is partially correct as to the 2008 opinion and only narrowly correct as to the 1993 opinion (which limits 'services' to 'professional services' and held dirt-hauling subject to mandatory bidding). Preserved as an interpretive caveat in the Rebid field, in Notes, and in Exceptions/Unresolved Issues. The current statutory text (last amended 2003) uses the bare word 'Services' with no qualifier, and Kansas school-district practice has applied (b)(1) to bus-services contracts — but a Kansas appellate decision specifically on point was not located.

**General state procurement.** K.S.A. 75-3739 (Department of Administration competitive procurement) reaches 'state agencies,' not school districts; K.S.A. 75-3317 defines USDs separately from state agencies. Therefore the state procurement code does not provide a parallel rule for USD bus contracts.

**Negative search paths.** Same as Stage 1, re-confirmed: no term cap or rebid mandate for private bus services located in Chapter 72 Article 64 (16 sections), Chapter 72 Article 11 (72-1149 lease cap excludes buses-as-lessor; 72-1151 services exemption; 72-1154 construction project delivery), Chapter 72 Article 67 (construction bidding only), Chapter 12 Article 29 (interlocal cooperation — permissive, no cap), Chapter 75 Article 37 (state-agency procurement does not apply), Chapter 10 Article 11 (cash-basis indebtedness restriction, not a term cap), or Chapter 72 Article 34 (special-ed definitional only).

**Tooling notes.** Direct WebFetch retrievals against ksrevisor.gov and ksag.washburnlaw.edu succeeded. ksde.gov returned TLS certificate verification errors from this environment for the Competitive Bidding Guidelines PDF (consistent with Stage 1). BoardDocs URLs (Andover, Pittsburg) returned HTTP 403 to general WebFetch. firecrawl MCP became unavailable mid-session due to token expiration. No essential PRIMARY authority remained unreachable — the statutes themselves were all re-opened directly on the official Revisor site.

**Orchestration provenance (this run).** Lane status: Stage 2 (adversarial validation) complete for Kansas. Per-field verdicts recorded in perFieldVerdicts. Sources re-verified vs. unreachable recorded in sourcesReVerified / sourcesUnreachable arrays.

sourceValidationStatus: verified — for the load-bearing primary citation behind Max-Term, Rebid, and Renewal (K.S.A. 72-1151), re-opened directly on https://ksrevisor.gov/statutes/chapters/ch72/072_011_0051.html on 2026-06-23 and confirmed verbatim text including subsection (a) sealed-bid scope, (b)(1) services exemption (bare word, no qualifier), (b)(4) motor-fuels exemption, (c) 1% in-district bidder preference, and source-note last-amendment L. 2003, ch. 53, § 2. All other load-bearing primary statutes (72-1149; 72-6487/6488/6489/6492/6498; 72-13,101; 10-1101/1112/1113; 72-3404) likewise re-opened and verified on the Revisor site. AG Opinions 1993-008 and 2008-009 re-opened on ksag.washburnlaw.edu and verified — note that AG 1993-008 narrowly construes (b)(1) services (preserved as interpretive caveat in Rebid finding, Notes, and Exceptions). Three secondary district-practice / guidance URLs (KSDE Competitive Bidding PDF, Andover BoardDocs, Pittsburg BoardDocs) were unreachable on re-verification (TLS / HTTP 403) and are flagged in sourcesUnreachable.
adversarialVerdictSummary: confirmed (5/6 fields); Rebid Required preserved as unresolved interpretive caveat (AG 1993-008 narrow 'professional services' reading vs. plain-text post-2003 'Services' / AG 2008-009 broader reading / uniform district practice) — bottom-line 'Not required' answer holds
